title: PyTorch Intro
content:
PyTorch Introduction

What is PyTorch?

PyTorch is an open-source machine learning library based on the Torch library, used for applications such as computer vision and natural language processing. It is primarily developed by Facebook's AI Research lab (FAIR).

Key Features

PyTorch provides two high-level features: Tensor computation (like NumPy) with strong GPU acceleration, and Deep neural networks built on a tape-based autograd system.

Example
import torch

# Check if PyTorch is installed
print(torch.__version__)

# Create a simple tensor
x = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
print(x)

title: PyTorch Tensors
content:
PyTorch Tensors

Creating Tensors

Tensors are the fundamental data structure in PyTorch, similar to NumPy arrays but with GPU support.

Common Tensor Creations

You can create tensors from data, or using built-in functions.

Tensor Examples
import torch

# From a list
data = [[1, 2],[3, 4]]
x_data = torch.tensor(data)

# Random and constant values
shape = (2,3,)
rand_tensor = torch.rand(shape)
ones_tensor = torch.ones(shape)
zeros_tensor = torch.zeros(shape)

print(f"Random Tensor: \n {rand_tensor} \n")
print(f"Ones Tensor: \n {ones_tensor} \n")
print(f"Zeros Tensor: \n {zeros_tensor}")

title: Tensor Operations
content:
Tensor Operations

Arithmetic Operations

Over 100 tensor operations, including arithmetic, linear algebra, matrix manipulation (transposing, indexing, slicing), sampling and more are comprehensively described in the PyTorch documentation.

Operations Examples

Each of these operations can be run on the GPU (at typically higher speeds than on a CPU).

Operation Examples
import torch

tensor = torch.ones(4, 4)
print(f"First row: {tensor[0]}")
print(f"First column: {tensor[:, 0]}")
print(f"Last column: {tensor[..., -1]}")

# Matrix multiplication
y1 = tensor @ tensor.T
y2 = tensor.matmul(tensor.T)

# Element-wise product
z1 = tensor * tensor
z2 = tensor.mul(tensor)

# Summation
agg = tensor.sum()
agg_item = agg.item()
print(agg_item, type(agg_item))

title: Autograd
content:
Autograd: Automatic Differentiation

What is Autograd?

`torch.autograd` is PyTorch’s automatic differentiation engine that powers neural network training. It records operations for computing gradients later.

Automatic Differentiation

When training neural networks, the most frequently used algorithm is back propagation. In this algorithm, parameters (weights) are adjusted according to the gradient of the loss function with respect to the given parameter.

Autograd Example
import torch

x = torch.ones(5)  # input tensor
y = torch.zeros(3)  # expected output
w = torch.randn(5, 3, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.randn(3, requires_grad=True)
z = torch.matmul(x, w)+b
loss = torch.nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(z, y)

# Compute gradients
loss.backward()
print(w.grad)
print(b.grad)

title: Neural Networks
content:
Neural Networks in PyTorch

Creating a Model

Neural networks can be constructed using the `torch.nn` package. An `nn.Module` contains layers, and a method `forward(input)` that returns the `output`.

Defining a Simple Network

Here is how you can define a simple feed-forward neural network.

NN Example
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(Net, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(784, 128)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, 10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.flatten(x, 1)
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x

net = Net()
print(net)

title: Datasets & DataLoaders
content:
Datasets & DataLoaders

Data Handling

PyTorch provides two data primitives: `torch.utils.data.DataLoader` and `torch.utils.data.Dataset` that allow you to use pre-loaded datasets as well as your own data.

Loading a Dataset

`Dataset` stores the samples and their corresponding labels, and `DataLoader` wraps an iterable around the `Dataset` to enable easy access to the samples.

Data Loading Example
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from torchvision import datasets
from torchvision.transforms import ToTensor

# Download training data from open datasets.
training_data = datasets.FashionMNIST(
    root="data",
    train=True,
    download=True,
    transform=ToTensor(),
)

# Create data loaders.
train_dataloader = DataLoader(training_data, batch_size=64)

for X, y in train_dataloader:
    print(f"Shape of X [N, C, H, W]: {X.shape}")
    print(f"Shape of y: {y.shape} {y.dtype}")
    break

title: Training a Model
content:
Training a Model

The Training Loop

To train a model, we need a loss function and an optimizer. In each training iteration, the model makes a prediction, calculates the loss, and adjusts its parameters.

Optimization Example

We use `torch.optim` to update weights.

Training Loop Example
import torch.optim as optim

model = Net()
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, momentum=0.9)

def train(dataloader, model, loss_fn, optimizer):
    for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(dataloader):
        # Compute prediction error
        pred = model(X)
        loss = loss_fn(pred, y)

        # Backpropagation
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        if batch % 100 == 0:
            print(f"loss: {loss.item():>7f}")

title: Transformations
content:
Data Transformations

torchvision.transforms

Data does not always come in its final processed form that is required for training machine learning algorithms. We use transforms to perform some manipulation of the data and make it suitable for training.

Common Transforms

All torchvision datasets have two parameters -`transform` to modify the features and `target_transform` to modify the labels.

Transform Example
import torch
from torchvision import datasets
from torchvision.transforms import ToTensor, Lambda

ds = datasets.FashionMNIST(
    root="data",
    train=True,
    download=True,
    transform=ToTensor(),
    target_transform=Lambda(lambda y: torch.zeros(10, dtype=torch.float).scatter_(0, torch.tensor(y), value=1))
)

# ToTensor() converts a PIL image or numpy.ndarray into a FloatTensor.
# and scales the image's pixel intensity values in the range [0., 1.]

title: CNNs (ConvNets)
content:
Convolutional Neural Networks

What are CNNs?

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are a class of deep neural networks, most commonly applied to analyzing visual imagery.

CNN Layers in PyTorch

PyTorch provides layers like `nn.Conv2d`, `nn.MaxPool2d`, and `nn.AvgPool2d` for building CNNs.

CNN Example
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class ConvNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(ConvNet, self).__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 6, 5)
        self.pool = nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(6, 16, 5)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(16 * 4 * 4, 120)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(120, 84)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(84, 10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool(F.relu(self.conv1(x)))
        x = self.pool(F.relu(self.conv2(x)))
        x = x.view(-1, 16 * 4 * 4)
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x

title: Custom Datasets
content:
Creating Custom Datasets

The Dataset Class

A custom Dataset class must implement three functions: `__init__`, `__len__`, and `__getitem__`.

Implementation Example

This allows you to load data from any source (CSV, Folders, etc.) and apply transforms.

Custom Dataset Example
import os
import pandas as pd
from torch.utils.data import Dataset

class CustomImageDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, annotations_file, img_dir, transform=None, target_transform=None):
        self.img_labels = pd.read_csv(annotations_file)
        self.img_dir = img_dir
        self.transform = transform
        self.target_transform = target_transform

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.img_labels)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        img_path = os.path.join(self.img_dir, self.img_labels.iloc[idx, 0])
        image = read_image(img_path)
        label = self.img_labels.iloc[idx, 1]
        if self.transform:
            image = self.transform(image)
        if self.target_transform:
            label = self.target_transform(label)
        return image, label

title: Saving & Loading
content:
Saving & Loading Models

Persistence in PyTorch

You can save a model's state dictionary or the entire model. The state dictionary is generally preferred.

State Dict Operations

`state_dict` is a Python dictionary object that maps each layer to its parameter tensor.

Save/Load Example
import torch
import torchvision.models as models

model = models.vgg16(weights='IMAGENET1K_V1')

# Save model weights
torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'model_weights.pth')

# Load model weights
model = models.vgg16() # create empty model
model.load_state_dict(torch.load('model_weights.pth'))
model.eval() # set to evaluation mode

# Save entire model
torch.save(model, 'model.pth')
model = torch.load('model.pth')

title: GPU Acceleration
content:
GPU Acceleration with CUDA

Why use a GPU?

GPUs provide significant speedup for deep learning tasks by parallelizing matrix operations. PyTorch makes it easy to move data and models to the GPU.

Using Device

Always check if CUDA is available before trying to move objects to the GPU.

CUDA Example
import torch

# Check for GPU
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
print(f"Using {device} device")

# Move tensor to device
x = torch.rand(5, 3)
x = x.to(device)

# Move model to device
model = Net().to(device)

# Operations on GPU
y = torch.rand(5, 3).to(device)
z = x + y
print(z.device)

title: Transfer Learning
content:
Transfer Learning

What is Transfer Learning?

Transfer learning involves taking a pre-trained model (trained on a large dataset like ImageNet) and fine-tuning it for your specific task.

Fine-tuning a Model

You can freeze weights of early layers and only train the final layers for your task.

Transfer Learning Example
import torch.nn as nn
from torchvision import models

# Load a pre-trained model
model = models.resnet18(weights='IMAGENET1K_V1')

# Freeze all parameters
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Replace the final fully connected layer
# Parameters of newly constructed modules have requires_grad=True by default
num_ftrs = model.fc.in_features
model.fc = nn.Linear(num_ftrs, 2) # e.g., 2 classes

# Only the parameters of the final layer will be optimized
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.fc.parameters(), lr=1e-3, momentum=0.9)

title: RNNs & LSTMs
content:
Recurrent Neural Networks

Sequence Data

RNNs are used for sequence data like text, time-series, or audio. LSTMs and GRUs are variants that handle long-range dependencies better.

LSTM Implementation

PyTorch provides `nn.RNN`, `nn.LSTM`, and `nn.GRU` modules.

RNN/LSTM Example
import torch.nn as nn

class RNNModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, output_size):
        super(RNNModel, self).__init__()
        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
        self.rnn = nn.LSTM(input_size, hidden_size, batch_first=True)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden_size, output_size)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x shape: (batch, seq_len, input_size)
        out, (hn, cn) = self.rnn(x)
        
        # Take the output of the last time step
        out = self.fc(out[:, -1, :])
        return out

title: Advanced Optimizers
content:
Advanced Optimizers

Beyond SGD

While Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is effective, advanced optimizers like Adam and RMSprop often converge faster and require less hyperparameter tuning.

Using Adam Optimizer

Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) keeps track of both gradients and their squared values.

Optimizer Examples
import torch.optim as optim

# Adam Optimizer
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, betas=(0.9, 0.999))

# AdamW (Adam with Weight Decay)
optimizer = optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=0.001, weight_decay=0.01)

# Using a Learning Rate Scheduler
scheduler = optim.lr_scheduler.StepLR(optimizer, step_size=30, gamma=0.1)

# In training loop
# optimizer.step()
# scheduler.step()

title: Inference & Eval
content:
Model Evaluation & Inference

Evaluation Mode

It is crucial to set the model to evaluation mode before running inference. This disables layers like Dropout and BatchNorm.

Running Inference

Using `torch.no_grad()` disables gradient calculation, which saves memory and speeds up computation.

Evaluation Example
import torch

# Set model to evaluation mode
model.eval()

# Disable gradient computation
with torch.no_grad():
    for data, target in test_loader:
        output = model(data)
        test_loss += loss_fn(output, target).item()
        pred = output.argmax(dim=1, keepdim=True)
        correct += pred.eq(target.view_as(pred)).sum().item()

print(f"Accuracy: {100. * correct / len(test_loader.dataset)}%")

title: TorchScript & Export
content:
TorchScript & Exporting

Production PyTorch

TorchScript is a way to create serializable and optimizable models from PyTorch code. It can be run in high-performance environments like C++.

Tracing vs Scripting

`torch.jit.trace` records operations by running an example input, while `torch.jit.script` parses the actual Python code.

Export Example
import torch

model = Net()
example_input = torch.rand(1, 1, 28, 28)

# Trace the model
traced_script_module = torch.jit.trace(model, example_input)

# Save for C++ / Production
traced_script_module.save("traced_model.pt")

# Export to ONNX
torch.onnx.export(model, example_input, "model.onnx")

title: Course Summary
content:
PyTorch Complete Course Summary 🎉

You Did It! 🎉

You have successfully completed the PyTorch masterclass! You now have the skills to build, train, and deploy advanced deep learning models.

  • Fundamentals: Tensors, operations, and autograd engine.
  • Architecture: Building CNNs, RNNs, and custom models.
  • Advanced: Data transformations, transfer learning, and GPU acceleration.
  • Deployment: Saving, loading, and exporting models with TorchScript.

Deep learning is an ever-evolving field. Keep exploring new architectures and pushing the boundaries of AI!
